narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model estimated a 46.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use to ensure we admit students likely to succeed. This prediction draws from patterns in data about past students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to forecast bar exam outcomes. For you specifically, your LSAT score of 36 stood out as the strongest positive signal, pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass since it showed solid preparation potential compared to many who struggled. However, your Year 3 class rank in the 2nd decile—well below the 6th decile average among those who passed—had an even stronger counteracting influence, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance that often correlates with bar exam challenges. Your Year 1 class rank, also in the 2nd decile versus the typical 6th for passers, added further weight toward the failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic strength. While your middle family income aligns with over a third of successful students and your choice to study full-time matches nearly all who passed, these didn't outweigh the ranking and LSAT factors in the model's eyes. Overall, your profile compares to accepted applicants by having a slightly lower LSAT than their 37.3 average and significantly lower undergraduate GPA of 2.4 versus their 3.24, which amplified the impact of your class ranks. The model prioritizes these academic indicators because they have proven the most reliable predictors of first-time bar success in our data. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven call to protect your investment in legal education. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic performance through additional coursework or tutoring, and reapplying with an updated transcript that highlights upward trends. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these key areas, and admissions teams often value demonstrated improvement. You're clearly motivated, and with targeted steps, you can shift the odds in your favor for future opportunities.
